## The Aim

Firebase is a fantastic cloud platform provided by Google with almost all of the bells and whistles you might expect. The free usage tier it offers is especially nice since it's suitable for most personal projects and some small businesses. The features it offers include analytics and hosting services along with two things which are particularly relevant to today's post: a (NoSQL database) storage option called Firestore, and cloud Functions. For our example today, we'll consider a small business which allows users to order a product. They want to easily enable email notifications to inform them when customers have placed a new order.  

## How do we do it?

We're going to use the power of Firebase Functions, along with some JavaScript code, to get the job done. Let's first look at what we need:
- An application which allows the user to register an account and place an order
- A Firebase project
- An email account to be used to send the email notifications

We'll assume that the small business already has a web application setup which allows a user to register and place orders. We'll also assume they have two document collections setup in Firestore, one called users and the other called orders. Each document in the users collection has a unique ID (auto-magically generated by Firestore) and contains the personal details of the specific user (stored when they register). Each document in the orders collection contains the details of an order, along with a time-stamp and the ID of the user who created the order. 

First off, we'll install the Firebase CLI using `npm install -g firebase-tools`. Following this, we authorise the CLI to access our Firebase projects using the `firebase login` command and following the in-browser prompts. After that, we specify the relevant project (using the ID of the project from the Firebase console) by running `firebase use <project ID>`. Finally, we can initialise a Firebase Functions project using `firebase init functions` and selecting the JavaScript option. 

Now we're ready to consult the [Firebase documentation](https://firebase.google.com/docs/functions/database-events) and create a Firebase Function that triggers whenever an order is placed. We'll be writing our Node.js function inside `functions/index.js` (automatically generated by the `init` command) and it will monitor the orders collection in the Firestore for creation of a new document:

```
const functions = require('firebase-functions');

exports.orderPlaced = functions.firestore
    .document('orders/{orderId}')
    .onCreate((snap, context) => {
      const newOrder = snap.data();
      newOrder.id = snap.id;

      console.log(`New order (ID: ${newOrder.id}) for ${newOrder.items.length} items`);

      return Promise.resolve();
    });
```

Using the code above we can print out details about every new order that is created. This is great but it doesn't tell us anything about the user that created the order. To get this information, we need to find the document in the users collection with an ID that matches the one in the new order:

```
const functions = require('firebase-functions');
const admin = require('firebase-admin');

admin.initializeApp();

exports.orderPlaced = functions.firestore
    .document('orders/{orderId}')
    .onCreate((snap, context) => {
        const newOrder = snap.data();
        newOrder.id = snap.id;

        db.doc(`userProfile/${newOrder.uid}`)
            .get({ source: 'server' })
            .then(user => {
              const userDetails = user.data()

              console.log(`New order (ID: ${newOrder.id}) for ${newOrder.items.length} items`);
              console.log(`The items should be delivered to ${userDetails.address}`);
            });

        return Promise.resolve();
    });
```

We're doing great. For every new order that is created we can print out information about order itself as well as the user that made it. Now we have the final step, actually emailing our business owners.

<describe email>:

```
<email code>
```

If you wanted to make this neater and more configurable, you could extract the email addresses and email template to a separate Firestore collection which gets loaded in when the function is triggered. To finish off we'll deploy our work using `firebase deploy --only functions`. If you navigate to the Functions tab in the Firebase console, you should see your brand new function up and running. You can also get diagnostic information about the function and view any logs. 

We made it! The business owners now get an email every time a new order is created with some information about the email and the user who made it. I hope this post was useful and that you learnt just how powerful Firebase Functions can be. I encourage you to play around with them and build something cool for your next project.

_Jason Parry_
